nativeRange.habitat: Small, isolated forest pools and slow-moving tributary streams in the Río Calima headwaters; soft, tannin-stained blackwater with dense riparian vegetation and leaf litter

decorPreferences: dense fine-leaved planting (Java moss, Cabomba, hornwort)
dark substrate (fine sand or dark gravel)
driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannins and cover
open swimming space in the centre with planted margins

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and granules as a staple. Eagerly takes small live and frozen foods: bloodworm (chironomid larvae), Daphnia, Artemia/brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, and tubifex. Varied diet including live or frozen items is important for maintaining vibrant coloration and conditioning for breeding.

avoidWith: slow-moving or long-finned fish susceptible to fin-nipping
very small nano fish or fry
boisterous or aggressive species that would outcompete them for food

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Nematobrycon lacortei; check availability and licensing before use.

summary: The Rainbow Tetra is a rare Colombian blackwater characid closely related to the Emperor Tetra (N. palmeri). Males are immediately distinguished by vivid red irises and blue-violet body with red/blue/green iridescence; females have bluish-green eyes. Seldom seen in trade; demands soft, acidic water and a spacious shoal.
careNotes:
Nematobrycon lacortei is an uncommon species in the aquarium hobby, typically available only as wild-caught imports from its restricted native range in the Río San Juan drainage (Choco and Valle de Cauca departments, western Colombia), specifically the Río Calima tributary. It is closely related to the Emperor Tetra (N. palmeri) and shares the same elongated caudal-fin extension of the middle rays in males. The two species are most reliably distinguished by eye colour: N. lacortei males have a bright red iris; N. palmeri males have a blue iris. Additional differences include N. lacortei's more saturated blue-violet dorsal colouration, scattered red/blue/green iridescence across the body and fins, a shorter and more triangular (rather than sickle-shaped) dorsal fin in males, and fewer pectoral-fin rays (9 vs. 10 in N. palmeri). Females of N. lacortei have bluish-green eyes and a fuller body, enabling sexing even in juveniles.

Water quality is the most critical care factor. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–8) closely replicating the species' blackwater biotope is strongly recommended; harder or more alkaline water is tolerated short-term but results in faded colour and reduced vitality. The addition of dried Indian almond (Terminalia catappa) leaves or peat filtration to produce mild tannin-staining is beneficial. Temperature should be maintained at 22–28°C.

A group of at least 8–10 individuals is recommended to distribute intermale territorial behaviour and produce the natural shoaling display. Males may spar with extended fins but rarely cause injury in adequately sized tanks. A 80-litre tank (minimum 80 cm footprint) suits a small shoal; 100–120 L is preferable for a larger group or a community setup. Dense planting along tank margins with open midwater swimming space replicates the natural habitat.
breedingNotes: Breeding is achievable in a well-conditioned species tank but is considered moderately challenging due to the requirement for very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5, TDS <100 ppm) and the tendency of adults to consume eggs and fry. Condition a pair or small group with live and frozen foods for 2–4 weeks before moving to a separate 20–40 L breeding tank furnished with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, Cabomba) or spawning mops. Eggs are scattered among plants; spawning typically occurs in the morning. Remove adults promptly after spawning to protect eggs. Eggs are light-sensitive — keep the breeding tank in subdued light or darkness until fry are free-swimming. Hatching occurs within 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming 4–5 days later and initially require infusoria or commercial fry foods, graduating to microworms and Artemia nauplii within the first week.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nematobrycon lacortei
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Río Calima, Río San Juan drainage, Colombia), temperature 20–28°C, pH 5.0–7.2, hardness 18–179 ppm, minimum tank dimensions 120×30×30 cm, diet (live/frozen micro-foods plus dried), temperament, tankmates, and breeding notes (hatching 24–36 h, free-swimming day 4–5, infusoria first food).

additional.name: FishBase — Nematobrycon lacortei
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.6 cm SL), temperature 23–27°C, pH 5.6–7.2, hardness up to 12 dH, native range (San Juan River basin, South America), taxonomy (Characiformes), IUCN Data Deficient status (2014), and trophic level 3.0.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — All Hail the Emperor Tetras
additional.notes: Provided species-level morphological detail for N. lacortei: male red iris vs female greenish-blue iris, deep-purple dorsal colouration in males, dark-blue-to-black lateral stripe with scattered reds/blues/yellows on body and fins, male size approximately 1¾ in (4.5 cm). Confirmed N. lacortei is considerably smaller and more vividly coloured than N. palmeri.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Rainbow Tetra (Nematobrycon lacortei)
additional.notes: Confirmed species is rarely seen in the aquarium hobby and typically available only as wild-caught specimens. Provided temperature 73–80°F (23–27°C), pH 5.6–7.2, hardness 1–12°H, lifespan 4–6 years, minimum 20-gallon tank, and distinguishing features (red-banded eyes, triangular dorsal fin vs sickle-shaped in Emperor Tetra).

additional.name: Aquadiction — Rainbow Tetra Species Spotlight
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Río Calima, Río San Juan, western Colombia — Choco and Valle de Cauca), adult size up to 4 cm, lifespan up to 5 years, pH 5.0–7.0, gH 5–8, temperature 22–26°C, minimum group size 8+, and breeding notes (egg scatterer, dim light required, 24–36 h hatching, fry free-swimming day 4–5).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer in fine-leaved plants. Requires very soft acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5) and conditioned adults. Adults will consume eggs; remove parents after spawning. Eggs light-sensitive; keep tank dark until fry are free-swimming. Hatching in 24–36 h; fry free-swimming day 4–5. First foods: infusoria, then microworms and Artemia nauplii.
